Title: Possessing the Gates of the Enemy: An Intercessionary Prayer Manual
Author: Cindy Jacobs

Title: His Last Bow
Author: Arthur Conan Doyle
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.

Title: The Last Dragon King (Kings of Avalier, #1)
Author: Leia Stone
My Review: I really enjoyed this book up to about 85% and then the drama introduced got to be very stressful for me. It was of a nature I did not enjoy. However, I have to say in redemption of the book, it does have a happy ending, so I can forgive it's complications. It does have some sex scenes which I glossed over. I don't enjoy reading erotica, so be aware, if you're like me. Overall though, great read. I'd tentatively recommend it, provided you can stomach some relationship stress. I am a very sensitive person, I suppose.

Title: Half a Soul (Regency Faerie Tales, #1)
Author: Olivia Atwater
My Review: This book was a delightful cute romance in a regency setting. No erotica, just amazingly cute romance. And the romance felt full and meaningful because it wasn't the book's only plot. There are other plots as well woven throughout that bolster and add strength to the romance plot. I highly enjoyed it. Wonderful read.

Title: Waiting on God: Spending Time in His Presence in Silence, Stillness & Expectation
Author: Michael Van Vlymen
My Review: This book gives practical tips on how to experience the Lord by waiting in His Presence in stillness and holy expectation. Michael's books such as "How to See in the Spirit" have led me on a journey of more of God, and I cannot recommend them enough. There are a few typos, but it's easy for me to look past that, considering how much this book feeds the spirit and has helped me.

Title: A Guide for Listening and Inner-Healing Prayer: Meeting God in the Broken Places
Author: Rusty Rustenbach

Title: Specter of the Past (Star Wars: The Hand of Thrawn, #1)
Author: Timothy Zahn
My Review: A book by Zahn... need I say more?  It follows out some of the ripples that Thrawn's life continues to exert... he casts a large shadow.

Title: Around the World in Eighty Days
Author: Jules Verne
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.

Title: From the Earth to the Moon and 'Round the Moon
Author: Jules Verne
My Review:
**This book is not the same edition that I read. I read it as part of "The Works of Jules Verne" large volume.

This book was not nearly as exciting as some of Verne's other novels.

Title: 31 Days of Praise: Enjoying God Anew
Author: Ruth Myers
My Review: This book has prayers of praise written for each day of the month and it's really helpful to making one's own praises.

Title: Powerful Keys to Spiritual Sight: Effective Things You Can Do To Open Your Spiritual Eyes
Author: Michael Van Vlymen
My Review: I really enjoyed this short version of Michael's longer book.  It encapsulated a lot of the important points.

Title: Furies of Calderon (Codex Alera, #1)
Author: Jim  Butcher
My Review: This book has exceptional characters and great plot.  One couldn't ask for more in a good story.

Title: Gazing Into Glory: Every Believer's Birth Right to Walk in the Supernatural
Author: Bruce D. Allen

Title: He Is There and He Is Not Silent
Author: Francis A. Schaeffer

Title: The Manifest Presence of God
Author: Walter Beuttler
My Review: I simply loved this collection of sermons by Walter Beuttler.  The cost of following Jesus is high, but it's worth it.

Title: How To See In The Spirit
Author: Michael R. Van Vlymen
My Review: This book was recommended to me by Brenda Cobb Murphy, and caused a shift in my thinking.  Firstly, it showed me that supernatural things really were open to all believers and one can walk in the supernatural as part of one's daily living.  It doesn't have to be a one time thing.  Secondly, it showed me that the supernatural is not evil - it's seeking it APART FROM JESUS - that is evil.  With Jesus, He can do all things in you for ministry, and none of it is bad.  That was also good to realize.  I highly recommend this book, especially to anyone who like myself wants to see angels someday.

Title: The Return of the King (The Lord of the Rings, #3)
Author: J.R.R. Tolkien
My Review: There are few books with such beauty and full of epic lore as this one.  The imagery that Tolkien uses is without copy.  The story is as epic as one could ever wish.  I had a great time reading it, and the ending was as beautiful as a fairy tale, yet with more substance.

Title: Seeing the Supernatural: How to Sense, Discern and Battle in the Spiritual Realm
Author: Jennifer Eivaz
My Review: This book describes the gift "discerning of spirits," mentioned in 1 Cor. 12:10, how to use it effectively in God's Kingdom, and how to wisely and prayerfully deal with what is discerned.  I really enjoyed this book.

Title: Lord of Chaos (Wheel of Time, #6)
Author: Robert Jordan
My Review: **Date finished reading is an approximation, but accurate within a month.

Title: Edgedancer (The Stormlight Archive, #2.5)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review: This story is amazing - hilarious - funny - and down-right unexpectedly surprising, just as always Sanderson manages in a good story.  There are some Sanderson stories that are so-so.  This is not one of those stories.  Lift takes it away with pranks, plot and more as she hunts her nemesis Darkness.  Not to mention - this story sheds loads of light on what is going on in Roshar after Words of Radiance.  A truly must read.

Title: Arcanum Unbounded: The Cosmere Collection
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review: I had already read The Emperor's Soul, but I listened to it again in this collection and fell in love with it again.  I loved the introduction to each story, in which a narrator explained its place in the cosmere. My favorite story is Edge Dancer, the last one, placed in Roshar.  The intro to Roshar was fantastically interesting.  However, one of the most intriguing stories that shed the most light on the cosmere as a whole was probably The Secret Histories.  That was fantastic and amazing.  I loved seeing what had happened to Kelsier after his death and what he had been doing while the rest of the story went on.  It also shed loads of light on the Stormlight Archive and Sanderson's whole universe.  What a great collection!  I highly recommend this series as a must read for any Sanderson lover.

Title: Dawn of Wonder (The Wakening, #1)
Author: Jonathan Renshaw
My Review: This book was slow in places, but had other parts that were fascinating.  I loved its conclusion.  It was a rambling, fun adventure with memorable and living characters.  The characters by far made the book, more than the plot.  I would recommend it to anyone who wants a nice, quiet and relaxing read by a fireside at night (or in the car) for fun.

Title: The Wild Romancer: Uncovering the Romance Jesus Longs To Lavish On You
Author: Brenda Cobb Murphy
My Review: This is one of the best books I've read - why?  Because it describes some of the life experiences of someone in love with Jesus, and some tips on how one can develop that kind of relationship with Him - it's thoroughly priceless.

Title: The Emperor's Soul
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review: This book is a great short read! It's about a forger who can rewrite the history of objects to change them into something else. She is captured and forced to reforge not an object but a soul - the Emperor - who was wounded in the head and died, yet still lives without mind. After this she is to be killed. Can she do it before the deadline and still escape?

Title: Intimacy with Christ: Her Letters Now in Modern English
Author: Jeanne Guyon
My Review: I love Guyon's letters - they contain nuggets of wisdom that can be re-read again and again.

Title: The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes
Author: Arthur Conan Doyle
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.

Title: The Intercessors Handbook: How to Pray with Boldness, Authority and Supernatural Power
Author: Jennifer Eivaz
My Review: This book was great - I identified with it a lot - and it helped me see that I was not as crazy as I had once believed.  It gave me a lot of food for thought on prayer as well.  I'd highly recommend it to all intercessors and prayer warriors.

Title: Since Nobody's Perfect, How Good is Good Enough?
Author: Andy Stanley

Title: Experiencing the Depths of Jesus Christ (Library of Spiritual Classics, Volume 2)
Author: Jeanne Guyon
My Review:
This book describes how to launch out in the deep with Jesus - how to go from being a new convert to the faith, to one who dives deeply into an experiential relationship with Jesus. The appendix at the end describes how this work has profoundly influenced such famous men as Zinzendorf and the Moravians, John Wesley and Watchman Nee. Indeed, almost every age has been touched by this book in some form or another, and it stands as a foundational piece in Christian literature.

I truly enjoyed reading this book - it talks about praying the Scripture, learning how to be silent before God, learning how to turn inwardly to Him and many other truly helpful topics. I think I will be coming back to this book a lot, as it is so deep, one cannot appreciate it all at once. It will also take me a long time to learn to practice what it describes, but I will make it one of my aims to try.

Title: Spiritual Torrents
Author: Jeanne Guyon
My Review: This book was fascinating, though the forward basically said not to read it.  I think it is important to remember when reading it that God works differently in the life of each believer, and not to try to judge oneself too much about where on the journey one is with God.  He performs all things in His time.  Nevertheless, it was a great read, and I recommend it.

Title: Hearing Heart
Author: Hannah Hurnard
My Review: This book gave me a picture of Hannah's walk with Jesus, and how she listened to Him, and learned to hear His Voice.  It was very insightful and full of the miracles that God did in her life.  I highly recommend it.  It's also a short read.

Title: Hinds' Feet on High Places
Author: Hannah Hurnard
My Review: This book gave me a better understanding of spiritual growth with the Lord than I have ever understood by a more textbook style means.  Hannah describes spiritual truth through story in a way that is truly enlightening.  Her depiction of the Lord was beautiful as well.  I found a lot of comfort, reading this work.

Title: Death Masks (The Dresden Files, #5)
Author: Jim  Butcher
My Review: *Date finished is guessed.

Title: Summer Knight (The Dresden Files, #4)
Author: Jim  Butcher
My Review: An excellent read!  I'm really enjoying the Dresden Files so far.  It's a great mix of the arcane with the modern world and Butcher makes it really fun, with the cool characters that get mixed up in these stories.  Dresden isn't a super hero - he bleeds, gets into impossible situations and gets hurt.  It makes it more real, suspenseful and relate-able.  Good stuff.

Title: Ben-Hur: A Tale of the Christ
Author: Lew Wallace
My Review: Very interesting story, with creative and fascinating blending with history.  A really fascinating read.  It gave me a different perspective on some things that I enjoyed.

Title: The Practice of the Presence of God
Author: Brother Lawrence
My Review:
This was one of those life changing books for me. It helped me see and desire a continual conversation with God, and see how simple living with Him is. It was encouraging and inspiring. I've tried to emulate much of what Brother Lawrence wrote about, and his simple life. He is right - it does get easier the more you practice. At ~50 pages, I highly recommend this book to everyone. It's a simple, easy and yet amazing read.

Read second time: October 12, 2015

Title: The Hound of the Baskervilles (Sherlock Holmes, #5)
Author: Arthur Conan Doyle
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.

Title: Summer Promise (Christy Miller, #1)
Author: Robin Jones Gunn
My Review: This book was ok; it was a love story.  But, it was never very exciting or interesting, in my opinion.  It was too ordinary.  I never read the rest of the series because of that.

Title: Shards of Time (Nightrunner, #7)
Author: Lynn Flewelling
My Review:
I would not recommend this book. I bought it for John because it intrigued me - someone being murdered by unseen forces out of a rift. I did not realize until I started reading it that it's likely the second book in a series. For all that, it's still quite understandable. However, it had a little too much evil, ghosts and necromancy for my taste. It ranks as the first book I've read with the two main characters being gay.

The plot is rather intriguing - ghost stories always are - as one wonders what ghosts and weird sights they will find next. Yet, there is an element of unbelievability that threw me out of the story as the characters did things that did not make sense.

Point 1: The govenor and his mistress were murdered gruesomely, reportedly by ghosts, that no one can do anything about. Let's go sleep in his bedchamber at midnight and SEE WHAT HAPPENS!


Point 2: A man disappeared mysteriously in a hallway and came back somewhere else, badly injured. Let's go SEARCH THAT HALLWAY AGAIN and see what happens!

Really? What are these people thinking, I wondered. I suspect horror type books are frequently like this, but I can't say for certain, as I don't read many. It turned out they were fighting a bad necromancer. There was one magician in their party. It was an ok book. But really, not worth the time.

Read something more interesting, people. Unless you really want to read a book about ghost stories and people doing dumb things. And it gave in some ways, a fairly accurate description of evil that was creepy. It creeped me out, until I got thrown out of the story by the dumb things the characters did.

Title: American Gods
Author: Neil Gaiman
My Review:
I have to say this for Gaiman - his use of metaphors is without parallel, and his descriptions as well. No one else writes like him. The ways he describes things are bizarre, but also apt, and gives a crystal clear picture of a thing, sound, smell, or sight in one's mind. There wasn't that much violence, but there were quite a few sex scenes - at least three - which I don't prefer. I enjoyed Neverwhere more, overall.

This was a good book - it was probably one of the strangest books I've ever read. And though it was interesting and even suspenseful, for some reason, there was a strange resistance while I read it. I found it difficult to finish. For most of the book, I did not understand what was going on or why. I think that was by design, in many ways.

I'm sure that there is a lot more meaning to this book than I am getting. It reminded me somewhat of Nietszche, mostly for the idea that human belief creates realities. There was a lot of metaphorical and interesting word plays - I really wish I was more familiar with the Greek and Roman classics.

Title: Alcatraz Versus the Evil Librarians (Alcatraz, #1)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review:
I loved the nonsense humor in this book. The magic system is pretty cool. People known as oculators can wear glasses that give them the ability to see new information about people and things. There are many different types of magical glasses that do different things depending on the glass they are made from. For instance, trackers lenses let one see footprints of people that have passed. The better you know someone, the longer the footprints stay.

In addition, the characters in this book have hilariously comical magical "talents" that seem like nonsense. For instance, one is magically good at breaking things. One is magically good at arriving late to things, another good at tripping, another at saying things that don't make sense. But it turns out that when you can "arrive late" to bullets being shot at you, these can become pretty useful. I loved seeing the uses that Sanderson found for these seemingly useless skills.

Meanwhile, the librarians have control of the United States and the known world, seeking power through information and pulling all the strings behind events. Some - those in the three Kingdoms - seek to end their tyranny and keep them from their lands. Alcatraz learns that he's part of a famous line of stubborn oculators, and goes on an adventure to take back the sands of Rashid, said to be able to make the most powerful lens of all time. He goes to great lengths, however, to prove that he's not the hero everyone thinks he is.

Title: The Pursuit of God (The Definitive Classic)
Author: A.W. Tozer

Title: The Rithmatist (Rithmatist, #1)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review: The Rithmatist is an alternate history - another masterful and fun piece by Sanderson with a great satisfying closer set around 1900.  In it, a sixteen year old boy named Joel grapples with not being a Rithmatist - having the power to draw chalk that comes alive.  People duel each other with chalk, building defenses, launching attacks and 2D creatures called "chalklings", all to prepare to fight at Nebrask.  Nebrask houses a tower full of wild chalklings that must be contained at all times, constantly surrounded and fought by Rithmatists.  Otherwise, they swarm out, crawl up people's skin and eat the flesh from their bones. Though not a Rithmatist, Joel finds himself in the center of an investigation to catch a killer.  Someone is capturing or killing teenagers - young Rithmatists - by swarming them with chalkings.  Why would anyone do such a thing?  Read the book to find out!  It's going to be a series and I highly recommend it.

Title: The Knowledge of the Holy
Author: A.W. Tozer
My Review: Tozer goes over, one by one, some of the attributes of God and it really gave me a lot of food for thought.  As he says, a low view of God is the cause of so many lesser evils and it really helped me think about God in new and higher ways than I previously have.  Trying to meditate on the reality of God is a brain buster but well worth doing.

Title: The Light Fantastic (Discworld, #2; Rincewind, #2)
Author: Terry Pratchett
My Review:
The Light Fantastic is great fun

This book is quite hilarious and is really written in nonsense style like Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy. I loved it! Books typically keep me up at night, but this was something silly that I could read and put down without fear of being too involved. There are no chapters. This bothered John, but I didn't even recognize that. Pratchett has written many, many excellent books and this one is his first.

What I know about the author

Pratchett is reknowned for writing excellent humor like Douglas Adams, while at the same time, managing to write engaging characters and plot. Only this one, his first book, is more toward the Douglas Adams side - pure nonsense - without that much plot mixed in. For this reason, I was told not to read it first, as he wrote much better in his later years and his books stand alone. Yet, I disagree - it depends on your purpose for reading and I wanted a silly book at night to read.

About the book

The book is about Rincewood, the worst wizard in the world, who begins the book by falling off the edge of it. In this universe, the world is called 'the Disc' and it is literally flat. My favorite character is the Luggage. Made of 'sapient pearwood,' it's a piece of magical luggage that follows its owner on hundreds of tiny legs with near fanatical loyalty. And it occasionally eats people it doesn't like. I highly recommend this book as a light, silly, relaxing read, after a long day of seriousness that you want to get away from.

Title: Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince (Harry Potter, #6)
Author: J.K. Rowling

Title: The Circle Maker: Praying Circles Around Your Biggest Dreams and Greatest Fears
Author: Mark Batterson

Title: Star Wars: Jedi Search (The Jedi Academy Trilogy, #1)
Author: Kevin J. Anderson
My Review:
This book was really pretty good. It has a three prong story focusing on Han Solo going to Kessel, Leia as she tried to balance being a mother and politics, as well as Lando, hunting down a possible Jedi candidate in a blob racing/gambling arena, as well as Luke and Lando trying to rescue Han later after he disappeared.

Lando going to blob races was hilarious - yes, it's just like what it sounds - blobs of goo bred for racing and traversing and obstacle course. I wish such was real. I'm a huge fan of "blobs" as aliens in SF. Another reason to like Schlock Mercenary. But I'm getting side-tracked.

This book was not particularly fast-paced or extremely engaging. It's not a Zahn or Sanderson book, but it was good. A nice, pleasant, relatively slow read. I'd recommend it if you enjoy Star Wars and the adventures of its major characters.

The end, the rescue of Han, which turned out badly, and the discovery of some hidden Imperials was a nice twist, and was actually pretty cool.

Title: The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes (Sherlock Holmes, #9)
Author: Arthur Conan Doyle
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.

Title: Resident Mad Scientist (Schlock Mercenary, #6)
Author: Howard Tayler
My Review: Every Schlock book I read just gets better and better.  The date read is approximate.

Title: Draw the Circle: The 40 Day Prayer Challenge
Author: Mark Batterson
My Review: This book has changed my life and is a foundational book about the basics of prayer - I recommend it for believers at any level.  Date read is approximate.

Title: The Scrapyard of Insufferable Arrogance (Schlock Mercenary, #5)
Author: Howard Tayler
My Review: Amazing!!! It gets better and better.

Title: 7 Truths from Experiencing God
Author: Henry T. Blackaby
My Review:
Great and very short (60 pgs) read. God used it to speak right to where I have been at. Highly recommended. But more than that, listen to what the Lord is trying to show you. It might be different.

Key Points:

1. Look for what God is doing around you and join Him in it.
2. There are some things only God can do, including drawing people to Himself. If you see that, you can know it is His work.
3. God often puts you somewhere not so that you can do something, but to show you what He is already about to do.
4. When God shows you something, He has already decreed it to happen. What He starts, He finishes.

Title: The Valley of Fear
Author: Arthur Conan Doyle
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.  Part 1 was much more interesting than part II.

Title: Destiny's Flower (Saldiora Book 1)
Author: Linda Harley
My Review: This book started out oddly, but I fell in love with it.  The plot really kept me going. The characters are lovable and heart-felt.  It was definitely a gripping tale.  The only thing that's driving me nuts is that the book ended on a cliff-hanger, when I thought it would be answering a lot more questions.

Title: Shades of Milk and Honey (Glamourist Histories, #1)
Author: Mary Robinette Kowal
My Review:
One of the best romances I've read. At first, I was a little peeved with how closely some of the characters and plot points followed Pride and Prejudice. There are only two daughters, instead of five - their mother is a hypochondriac, and their eldest daughter is even named Jane. However, as I kept reading, things diverged considerably. Not only were there two "darcy-like" characters, but the plot became very different. Also, the magic system is amazing and fits quite well in the 1800s. It's an illusion magic that allows them to decorate their homes with life-like illusory trees and birds, etc. It's kind of like an extended form of painting.

The book was so gripping that after two days of reading it at night, I read the rest of it in one day and could not put it down. It was far more gripping in the way it snags you than Pride and Prejudice, which I consider more of a slow and warming burn.

I highly recommend this book, and I have already ordered the sequel, Glamour and Glass. Have no fear though - you might think this book will leave you hanging, but it doesn't. It's very satisfying. :)

Title: The Economy of God
Author: Witness Lee
My Review: This is the best spiritual book that I have *ever* read.  It gives the most clear picture I have seen of God's mission/purpose on earth, and what it means to walk with God in the Spirit practically.  I highly recommend this book for as a must-read for every believer in Jesus.  You won't regret it.

Title: Basic Elements of the Christian Life, Volume Two
Author: Witness Lee & Watchman Nee

Title: The Blackness Between (Schlock Mercenary, #4)
Author: Howard Tayler
My Review: Schlock's books are hilarious and awesome - I've barely got into the "good stuff" yet of the series, but this book cracked me up.  So many great laugh-out-loud moments - Kevin continues to amuse; Breya is back and so much more!

Title: Under New Management (Schlock Mercenary, #3)
Author: Howard Tayler
My Review:
Schlock Mercenary is so much fun - this one was no exception. It's quite short, but worth it. The next one is short as well, and then it's on to the longer comic books. Schlock's and Kevin's antics are my favorite. Capt. Tagon is hilarious... Howard Taylor does such as awesome job with character. Some of my favorite strips in this one are on p. 49 about the very large telescope made out of their entire terapedo arsenal.

I like to think of this series as a funnier Brewster Rockit Space Guy with better characters and a real story! Plus, Sgt. Schlock is a carbosilicate amorph, a.k.a. sentient ball of goo... which is totally one of my favorite concepts in SF! >.>

Title: Legion (Legion, #1)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review: I didn't know Sanderson could write short books!  >.>  This one was only 75 words long, and it was awesome!  Great characters - great concepts.  A little mystery/thriller with a big boom.  Finished it in one day.  I *highly* recommend it!  You can get this one more easily as an ebook.  I was lucky enough to pick up a signed hard back copy of it at Dragon*Con.  :)

Title: The News Quiz: The Very Best of 2004
Author: Charles Dickens
My Review: The book in the beginning was a bit slow and it took me a while to get into it, but I really started enjoying after I passed the half-way point.  It is certainly the best audio dramatization that I have ever listened to.  I was surprised that the ending was relatively happy.  My only regret in listening to audio books is that I cannot see the spelling of unusual words as I come across them.

Title: The Sign of Four (Sherlock Holmes, #2)
Author: Arthur Conan Doyle
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.

Title: Dune
Author: Frank Herbert
My Review: *The date read for this book is not exact.  Excellent book - full of intrigue and lore.  It definitely has en epic feel.  The culture was extremely well-developed, intricate and fascinating.  I thought the ending though was rather sad.

Title: The Teraport Wars (Schlock Mercenary, #2)
Author: Howard Tayler
My Review: *The date read is not exactly.  This book is *awesome* and completely hilarious!  I loved every bit of it.  It has the perfect mix of humor, surprise, unexpected plot twists and bumps in the road.  Highly recommended.

Title: The Two Towers
Author: J.R.R. Tolkien
My Review: I loved The Two Towers - such earthy, yet beautiful and poetic description Tolkien gives.  I listened to the cd book read by Rob Inglis, but I cannot find that edition on Goodreads.  I highly recommend it.  Faramir was even more noble and wise here, and to my amazement, I got to the part where Frodo was captured by the orcs!  There's hardly any tale left to tell!  I can't wait to read The Return of the King.

Title: Deceived (Star Wars: The Old Republic, #2)
Author: Paul S. Kemp
My Review: Good book - interesting, and managed to keep me guessing sometimes.  A good ending, bitter sweet.  It does not focus as much on the destruction of Coruscant as I thought. It's mostly about what happens afterward, and why Malgus made the choices that he did.

Title: The Hunger Games (The Hunger Games, #1)
Author: Suzanne Collins
My Review: This book was about exactly what it sounded like it was about - very tragic, gory and somewhat disturbing.  All in all, it was a good read, but it left me feeling depressed.  Though the ending was good, it was sad. Not sure that I'd recommend it, per se.

Title: Revan (Star Wars: The Old Republic, #1)
Author: Drew Karpyshyn
My Review: Very interesting!  Tells what happens to Revan after KOTOR I and II.  Yes, his story is sad, but how can one not read it anyway?  It's Revan!  He's probably my favorite Star Wars character outside of Admiral Thrawn.  I do recommend it.  Revan does not betray Bastila.  You can rest easy about that.

Title: The Name of the Wind (The Kingkiller Chronicle, #1)
Author: Patrick Rothfuss
My Review:
Excellent book - why? - 1) expertly written - Rothfuss's word choices and descriptions rival the best 2) gripping - the story keeps one in serious suspense 3) great characters - all the characters in this book really come alive 4) great magic system 5) I like how he pokes fun at heroic stories saying things like, "but it didn't happen like you it does in stories" etc.

The overall story is gripping, overtones of sadness and pain, with humor thrown in. The ending is good. It deserves five stars - the only reason I gave it 4 was that it was a tad more stressful than I enjoy reading. Worrying about whether or not a character is going to ruin in life because of his flaws is not my favorite kind of stress. Nevertheless, I loved this book anyway.

Rothfuss is on par with Brandon Sanderson. This is extremely high praise from me. I highly recommend it.

Title: Crazy Love: Overwhelmed by a Relentless God
Author: Francis Chan
My Review: I really enjoyed this book - it challenged me to live out my faith and spoke of the hypocrisy in many churches.  I recommend it to everyone!

Title: The Fellowship of the Ring (The Lord of the Rings, #1)
Author: J.R.R. Tolkien
My Review: I really enjoyed listening to this book - it's a perfect listening story - not so suspenseful that one is tempted to stay in the car and listen to it when one should be working; but not boring either - a wonderful, delightful tale that slowly unfolds as one listens.  I love the songs in here too - another reason why listening is great, because Inglis sings them.  I hope I can listen to them all again and memorize them.  I like them all!  So much beauty.  So much history.  So much tragedy, yet hope.  A truly beautiful story.

Title: Neverwhere (London Below, #1)
Author: Neil Gaiman
My Review: Neverwhere is a fascinating book and one of the most unusual I've read recently.  My favorite aspect aspect of it is all the very odd metaphors that Gaiman comes up with to describe things - I don't think I've ever read the like!  Also, great adventure and an interesting/unexpected ending.  The world he creates for London Below is truly intriguing and quite bizarre.  I would recommend it, as long as one does not mind some pretty nasty villains - Croup and Vandemar.  They were some of the most memorable and horrifying villains I've read in a long time!

Title: The Deerslayer
Author: James Fenimore Cooper
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.

Title: The Last Battle (Chronicles of Narnia, #7)
Author: C.S. Lewis
My Review: The most beautiful book I have ever yet read!  I listened to the audio version.

Title: Alcatraz Versus the Scrivener's Bones (Alcatraz vs. the Evil Librarians, #2)
Author: Brandon Sanderson

My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.  One of the things that stood out to me about it is the beauty with which it is written.  The quality is unbelievable.  Even though the story was not particularly riveting, somehow, I could not help but to enjoy it and keep reading.  I felt like I was reading poetry in prose.  "Now this," I remember thinking, "is a real book."  I wish more modern novels had the intense quality of this book.  As you read it, one can almost feel your brain cells expanding.

Author: Anonymous
My Review: I wanted to read the JPS version, and found it was quite good.  It did not differ much from KJV in most respects.  I enjoyed trying to read some of the Hebrew, found out new things about Psalms, and enjoyed learning the different order of the books.  After reading this book, I occasionally started trying to read other books from the wrong side by accident.

Title: The Holy Bible: King James Version
Author: Anonymous
My Review: **I did not read this exact version.  I reread the Bible through a second time in the summer of 2010, reading the New Testament in KJV and the Old Testament in the JPS Hebrew-English pocket Tanakh.  There is so much to understand within these pages, that I could study them for millions of years and never learn it all.

My Review: I loved the character Zed most in this book.  The ending was great.  John read the entire thing to me.

Author: Anne Frank
Additional Authors: Eleanor Roosevelt, Susan Massotty, Γιάννης Θωμόπουλος, Barbara Mooyaart-Doubleday, Otto H. Frank, Mirjam Pressler

Title: Voyage of the Dawn Treader (Chronicles of Narnia Series #5)
Author: C.S. Lewis
My Review: I was surprised how much I loved this book.  It follows the random adventures of the Dawn Treader.  As I listened to it, I enjoyed it more and more.  The end was one of the best I've ever read, not for its amazing literary quality, but just because I loved it and it was very significant to me personally.  Favorite parts:  the story of Eustace, the Sea People, Ramandu, and of course, the end.  This story also references dragons and what "everyone" knows about them.  I must find those stories.  And I cannot wait to read The Silver Chair!  I'm wondering who will be in it.  My guess is Eustace.

Title: The Hobbit
Author: J.R.R. Tolkien
My Review: Do not think I hated this book, just because I'm only giving it three stars.  It was a bit slow initially, but the rich story and details were fascinating.  I especially enjoyed what I learned about the elves and dragons.  Written for children, the narration was entertainly different from what I've been reading lately.  Tolkien references "other dragon stories" when speaking of Smaug, and how the reader has doubtless heard about what dragons are like from them.  However, this is only the second book I've read that mentioned dragons.  In the first, Goodkind's The Sword of Truth, they did not play a large role.  This fact has made me even more curious to read some of the first great stories out there about dragons and other tales, such as King Arthur and books by George MacDonald.  Additionally, this book really helped me understand the first book of The Lord of the Rings better.  It amused me how the Ring was not in the spotlight of this story.

Title: The Giver (The Giver, #1)
Author: Lois Lowry
My Review: I had many recommendations for this book, so I picked it up.  It was a great and unusual story.  In many places were it could have been vulgar or graphic, it was not, which surprised me.  The ending also surprised me.  It is one of the shortest books I've read recently, thus the pacing was much different, which intrigued me.  Even though it is so short, it does a remarkable job of story development.  I recommend it to anyone looking for a quick and fun read.

Title: The Bourne Identity (Jason Bourne, #1)
Author: Robert Ludlum
My Review: **I listened to the CD version.  This book was ok, but the plot consisted almost completely of action, suspense and mystery, no real character development.  Thus, it felt a bit two dimensional to me.  Also, it had an intense amount of profanity.  Especially because of the latter, I could not really enjoy it.

My Review: Bijoy gave me this book to read when I was having trouble with PCR.  I read it, and did learn a lot about the theory behind PCR that was quite interesting.  It was rather dry though.

Title: The Communist Manifesto
Author: Karl Marx
My Review: **Date finished reading is an approximation. Ok, this book rating requires explanation.  I absolutely hated Marx, and his Manifesto absolutely infuriated me the first time I read it.  However, the second time I read it I was more reasonable and stopped hating it just because.  He does make some valid points in places.  I just think the conclusions he comes to are completely off base for specific reasons.  I recommend that everyone read it.  It's important to understand why you disagree with someone, and not just blindly hate them.  These days, I merely dislike Marx intensely, but I don't hate him virulently.  The date listed here is of the first reading.

Title: The First and Second Discourses
Author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
Additional Authors: Judith R. Masters, Roger D. Masters
My Review: **Date finished reading is an approximation. Rousseau was rather entertaining, if only because it was difficult to tease out what he was saying, but I am not a huge fan of him.  He tends to get on my nerves sometimes.  Nevertheless, I need to read more of his books.  They were important influences on many other authors.  It was fascinating to me to see what he really said, versus what I'd always been told he said.  The idea of the "noble savage" is largely false - his savages were not noble.

Title: The Prince
Author: Niccolò Machiavelli
Additional Authors: Rufus Goodwin, Benjamin Martinez, W.K. Marriott, Denis Daly, Μαρία Κασωτάκη
My Review: **Date finished reading is an approximation. I agreed with nothing in The Prince, except maybe a quote here or there, but really enjoyed reading it.  Dr. Shapiro's discuss of it in his modern politics class was fascinating too.  It was hilarious to me at every turn, for some reason.  I need to read it again.

Title: Waking the Dead: The Glory of a Heart Fully Alive
Author: John Eldredge
My Review: Waking the Dead was and still is a life changing book for me.  I recommend it to everyone.  In it, Eldredge discusses the four streams:  walking with God, fellowship, healing and spiritual warfare and how they all need to be combined into one unit in one's spiritual life.  It's a very deep and refreshing book - there's so much more that he says that is so important I cannot write here.  My only recommendation to you is to read his works as soon as possible.  He has been a major influence upon me.

Title: The Office of Assertion: An Art of Rhetoric for the Academic Essay
Author: Scott F. Crider
My Review: This was an amazing book, a short read, and provided stimulating and fascinating commentary about how to truly write well, and about what rhetoric really means.  In short, I completely loved it.

Title: Leviathan
Author: Thomas Hobbes
My Review: **This book is not the same edition that I read.  I only read Parts 1 and 2.  Hobbes is really interesting to me.  A bit verbose and self-important writing style, but I enjoy his logical mindset.  Thinking over his conclusions and reasoning concerning government and comparing them to John Locke is immensely entertaining.  I still haven't decided what to think of much of what he said.

Title: Mistborn: The Final Empire (Mistborn, #1)
Author: Brandon Sanderson
My Review: This book was unexpected, shockingly good.  Brandon Sanderson, a relatively new author in fantasy who has taken over the final book writing for the Wheel of Time Series, is a truly great novelist in his own right.  His books are fast-paced, keep you guessing and are amazingly good quality!  A must read.

My Review: **From this book, I read Thus Spoke Zarathustra - so I did not literally read all of it.  It was very intriguing in places, odd, definitely confusing and disturbing.  I read it because I would like to understand Nietzsche better, since he is one of the greatest influences on modern thought.

Title: Maran Illustrated Cooking Basics

My Review: I love the excellent pictures, easy recipes and simple instructions in this book.  It made reading a cook book fun.

My Review: The first book to Jordan's Wheel of Time series, probably the most fantastic story in fantasy:  unbelievably complex plot, realistic characters that will both infuriate you and make you laugh, and excellent description.  A must read.

My Review: This is a short read, and rather hilarious.  I really enjoyed it!  It's about the herd-like nature of people and how someone can unconsciously step out and lead the others without realizing it.

My Review: Octavia Butler is a strange writer, and poses some interesting philosophical questions in this book.  It is entertaining, but not the most interesting, and violent.  Overall, not my favorite.

Author: Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley
My Review: Single-handedly one of the most depressing books I've ever read.  Almost nothing good happened in it.  I suppose one could call it a modern tragedy, and it probably does deserve it's place as a classic.  Nevertheless, I could not stand to watch so much evil happen with absolutely no hope throughout.

My Review: Ender's Game was a good book, the plot was engaging, but it was too dark for my taste.  I believe all of Orson Scott Card's books have this in common: a thread of foreboding joylessness presses upon the reader.  For this reason, I do not truly enjoy his books, and have never been able to pick up the sequels of this one yet.

Title: Star Wars: The Last Command (Thrawn Trilogy, #3)
Author: Timothy Zahn
My Review: The third and last book of the Thrawn trilogy - it just continues to get better and better!  No one can tell me they hate Star Wars novels until they have read this series.  I'm serious when I contend that it is actually good literature.

Title: Star Wars: Dark Force Rising (Thrawn Trilogy, #2)
Author: Timothy Zahn
My Review: The second book to the Thrawn Trilogy continues the amazing trend that the first book started.  An absolute must read.  There are very few if any books that I reread, but this one (along with the rest of the Thrawn trilogy) will probably be one of them.

Title: Star Wars: Heir to the Empire (Thrawn Trilogy, #1)
Author: Timothy Zahn
My Review: Now here is a Star Wars book that is of real quality:  a complex, thrilling plot; one of the best characters in literature (Thrawn - and that's no joke), and good writing.  This book brings Star Wars novels to a whole new level.  Whatever you've heard about how junky Star Wars novels are, completely does not apply to this book.  Not only is it great story, it's great literature.  Zahn is simply amazing!  The characters he draws are incredibly well-done and the plot does not let up.  I would recommend this book and the rest of the Thrawn trilogy to be the first Star Wars novels people should pick up.

Title: The Centurions (The Centurions, #1)
Author: Damion Hunter
My Review: I would not recommend this book.  It was too vulgar for my taste in places.  However, it is a fascinating look at Roman history through fiction, which I did enjoy.  The Roman times fascinate me.
